title: Been vs gone: someone away
question: 'Tom has ___ to Brazil. He won't be back until Friday.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'gone' (past participle) with 'has'. 'Went' is past simple and cannot follow 'has'; 'goed' and 'wented' are not English words.

title: Been vs gone: someone returned
question: 'I've ___ to Brazil twice. It's a beautiful country.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'been' (past participle of 'be') with 'have'. 'Was' is past simple and cannot follow 'have'; 'beed' and 'wased' are not words.

title: Been vs gone: question form
question: 'Has she ___ to the dentist yet?' Which is correct?
explanation: Use 'been' (past participle) with 'has'. 'Wented', 'goed', and 'beed' are not English words.

title: For vs since: duration
question: 'We've lived here ___ fifteen years.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Since fifteen years', 'from fifteen years', and 'ago fifteen years' are all ungrammatical with present perfect duration. Use 'for' with a period of time.

title: For vs since: point in time
question: 'She's worked at the bank ___ she graduated.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'For she graduated', 'from she graduated', and 'ago she graduated' are ungrammatical. Use 'since' with a point in time or past event.

title: For vs since: tricky duration
question: 'I haven't eaten anything ___ this morning.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'For this morning', 'from this morning', and 'ago this morning' are ungrammatical with present perfect. 'This morning' is a point in time, so use 'since'.

title: For vs since: tricky period
question: 'He's been ill ___ a long time.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Since a long time', 'from a long time', and 'ago a long time' are ungrammatical. 'A long time' is a duration, so use 'for'.

title: Still/already/yet: negative still
question: 'She ___ replied to my message, even though I sent it hours ago.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Still' goes before 'hasn't' to show surprise at an ongoing absence. 'Hasn't still', 'yet hasn't', and 'already hasn't' are all ungrammatical word orders.

title: Still/already/yet: already in questions
question: 'Have you ___ finished?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Yetly', 'stilly', and 'alreadily' are not English words. 'Already' before the past participle shows surprise that something happened soon.

title: Still/already/yet: yet vs still
question: 'I haven't decided ___.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Yetly', 'stilly', and 'alreadily' are not English words. 'Yet' at the end of a negative present perfect sentence means 'up to now'.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: finished time
question: 'I ___ three cups of coffee yesterday morning.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Yesterday morning' is a finished past time, so use past simple 'had'. 'Have had' is present perfect; 'have been had' and 'having had' are wrong forms here.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: unfinished time
question: Which sentence describes an experience connected to the present?
correct: I have had two meetings this week.
wrong1: I have been had two meetings this week.
wrong2: I has have two meetings this week.
wrong3: I having had two meetings this week.
explanation: Present perfect 'have had' connects past actions to the present. The other forms are ungrammatical verb constructions.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: unfinished today
question: 'I ___ three strange emails so far today.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Have receive' (missing -ed), 'has received' (wrong subject agreement with 'I'), and 'have been receive' are all ungrammatical. Present perfect with 'so far today' requires 'have received'.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: just
question: 'Don't worry about dinner — I ___ already eaten.' Which is correct?
explanation: Use present perfect 'have eaten' for a recent completed action with present relevance. 'Did already eaten', 'am already eaten' and 'was already eaten' are ungrammatical forms here.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: result now
question: 'I can't find my keys. I ___ them.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Has lost' doesn't agree with 'I'; 'losed' is not a word (use 'lost'); 'have been lost' is passive. Present perfect 'have lost' shows a past action with a present result.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: when question
question: 'When ___ you start learning English?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Haved', 'doed', and 'wased' are not English words. Questions with 'when' about a specific past time use past simple 'did'.

title: Present perfect vs past simple: no time word
question: '___ you seen my phone anywhere?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Haved', 'haves' (with 'you'), and 'haveing' are all ungrammatical. With the past participle 'seen', use 'Have you seen' in present perfect.

title: Present perfect: lifetime experience
question: Which sentence describes a lifetime experience up to now?
correct: We have been to the gym three times.
wrong1: We been to the gym three times.
wrong2: We has been to the gym three times.
wrong3: We having been to the gym three times.
explanation: Present perfect requires 'have' + past participle. 'We been' is missing the auxiliary; 'has' doesn't agree with 'we'; 'having been' is a participle, not a finite verb.

title: Polite request: could
question: '___ you pass me the salt, please?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Coulded', 'coulding', and 'coulds' are not English words. 'Could you' is a standard polite request form.

title: Can vs could: past ability
question: 'When I was young, I ___ run very fast.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Can' is present tense and clashes with 'when I was young'; 'canned' and 'coulding' are not modal forms. Use 'could' for past ability.

title: Could: present possibility
question: 'It ___ rain later. Take an umbrella just in case.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Coulds', 'coulded', and 'coulding' are not English words. 'Could' expresses uncertain present possibility.

title: Must vs have to: obligation form
question: 'I really ___ study harder.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Musts', 'musted', and 'musting' are not English words. 'Must' is a modal and never takes -s, -ed, or -ing.

title: Must vs have to: past obligation
question: 'At my old school, we ___ wear a uniform every day.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Musted' is not a word; 'had must' and 'have to had' are ungrammatical. 'Must' has no past form, so use 'had to' for past obligations.

title: Don't have to: no necessity
question: 'You ___ shout; I can hear you perfectly well.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Don't must', 'haven't to', and 'not must' are all ungrammatical. The negative of 'have to' meaning 'not necessary' is 'don't have to'.

title: Mustn't: prohibition
question: 'You ___ touch that wire. It's dangerous!' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Musn't' is a misspelling; 'mustn't to' adds an incorrect 'to'; 'not must' is the wrong word order. 'Mustn't' expresses prohibition.

title: Modal form: may
question: '___ I use your phone, please?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Mays', 'mayed', and 'maying' are not English words. 'May' is a modal and never takes -s, -ed, or -ing.

title: Might: possibility form
question: 'She ___ have missed the bus.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Mighted', 'mighting', and 'mights' are not English words. 'Might' is a modal and is followed by the bare infinitive 'have'.

title: Must: logical deduction
question: 'He's been working for 12 hours. He ___ exhausted.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Must is', 'must being', and 'must to be' are ungrammatical. Modals like 'must' are followed by a bare infinitive: 'must be'.

title: Should: advice form
question: 'You look tired. You ___ go to bed early tonight.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Shoulds', 'shoulded', and 'shoulding' are not English words. 'Should' is a modal and never takes -s, -ed, or -ing.

title: Can't: logical impossibility
question: 'That ___ right. The math doesn't add up.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Can't is', 'can't being', and 'can't to be' are ungrammatical. Modals like 'can't' are followed by a bare infinitive: 'can't be'.

title: Going to: prediction form
question: 'She's driving far too fast. She ___ crash!' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Is going' is missing 'to'; 'going to' is missing the auxiliary 'is'; 'is go to' is ungrammatical. The full form is 'is going to' + bare infinitive.

title: Will: spontaneous decision form
question: 'The phone is ringing. I ___ answer it.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Willed', 'wills' (as a modal), and 'willing' are not modal forms. 'Will' is a modal and never inflects with -ed, -s, or -ing.

title: Going to: planned intention form
question: 'We ___ visit Grandma this weekend. We arranged it yesterday.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Are go to' is ungrammatical; 'is going to' doesn't agree with 'we'; 'are going' is missing 'to' before the infinitive. The full form is 'are going to visit'.

title: Going to: evidence-based form
question: 'Look! That tree is shaking. It ___ fall down!' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Is going' is missing 'to'; 'going to' is missing 'is'; 'are going to' doesn't agree with 'it'. The correct form is 'is going to' + bare infinitive.

title: Will: negative form
question: 'Don't ask him — he ___ help you. He's in a bad mood.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Willn't' is not a contraction in English; 'don't will' and 'isn't will' mix auxiliaries incorrectly. The negative of 'will' is 'won't'.

title: Going to: negative form
question: 'I ___ going to eat that — it smells strange.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Don't going to' and 'not am going to' are ungrammatical; 'amn't' is not standard English. The negative of 'am going to' is 'am not going to'.

title: Will: form with promise
question: 'I ___ help you with your homework after dinner.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Willed' is not a modal; 'am will' mixes forms; 'willing' is not a finite verb form here. 'Will' is the base modal.

title: Will: question form
question: '___ you be home before ten tonight?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Wills', 'do will', and 'are will' are ungrammatical combinations. Form will-questions with 'Will + subject + base verb'.

title: Present perfect: ever in questions
question: '___ she ever eaten octopus?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Haves', 'haved', and 'is ... eaten' are ungrammatical. With 'she', use 'Has' + past participle to ask about life experience.

title: Going to: intention form
question: 'I ___ learn to drive this summer. I've already booked lessons.' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Am go to' is ungrammatical; 'going to' is missing the auxiliary 'am'; 'am going' is missing 'to' before the bare infinitive 'learn'.

title: Question tag: positive to negative
question: 'You've been to London, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Haven't I' doesn't match the subject 'you'; 'don't you' uses the wrong auxiliary; 'hasn't you' has wrong agreement. Use 'haven't you' after 'you've'.

title: Question tag: negative to positive
question: 'She doesn't like coffee, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Do she' has wrong agreement; 'does her' uses the object form; 'doesn't she' is negative after a negative statement. Use positive 'does she'.

title: Question tag: with will
question: 'They'll come to the party, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Won't them' uses the object form; 'willn't' is not a contraction in English; 'doesn't they' has wrong auxiliary and agreement. Use 'won't they'.

title: Question tag: with can't
question: 'You can't swim, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Can't you' repeats the negative; 'can thou' is archaic/ungrammatical; 'cans you' is not an English form. Use positive 'can you' after the negative 'can't'.

title: Question tag: imperative
question: 'Close the door, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Will it', 'will them', and 'will he' use wrong pronouns or forms. For imperatives addressed to a listener, use 'will you'.

title: Question tag: nobody
question: 'Nobody called, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Did them' and 'did their' use wrong pronoun forms; 'didn't they' is negative after the negative 'nobody'. Use positive 'did they'.

title: Question tag: somebody
question: 'Somebody left their bag here, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Didn't them', 'didn't their', and 'didn't theys' use wrong pronoun forms. Use 'didn't they' after 'somebody' in a positive statement.

title: Question tag: I am
question: 'I'm right, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Amn't I' is not standard English; 'am not I' has wrong word order for a tag; 'aren't me' uses the object form. The fixed tag for 'I am' is 'aren't I'.

title: Question tag: let's
question: 'Let's go for a walk, ___?' Which is correct?
explanation: 'Shall us' uses the object form; 'shall they' has the wrong subject; 'shalls' is not a word. The fixed tag for 'let's' is 'shall we'.

title: Present perfect: past participle of 'take'
question: 'She has ___ the dog for a walk.' Which is correct?
explanation: The past participle of 'take' is 'taken' (irregular). 'Took' is past simple and cannot follow 'has'; 'taked' is not a word; 'taking' is a present participle.
